preamble.summary: A national lament following military defeat, attributed to David's wars against Aram and Edom. The psalm begins with anguished complaint that God has rejected and broken his people, then transitions into a divine oracle in which God claims sovereignty over the entire land, and concludes with renewed trust that God will grant victory despite apparent abandonment.
preamble.remarkable: This psalm contains one of the most striking divine speeches in the Psalter (vv. 8-10). God speaks from his sanctuary and divides the land like a general assigning territory: Shechem, Succoth, Gilead, Manasseh, Ephraim, Judah, Moab, Edom, Philistia — all are his to distribute. The language is deliberately domestic and even humorous: Moab is God's washbasin, over Edom he flings his sandal, and Philistia is told to shout in submission. The imagery reduces Israel's most feared enemies to household objects and servants. God is not intimidated by geopolitics; he treats international powers the way a householder treats his washbasin and his shoes. The theological audacity is breathtaking — and it comes in a psalm of defeat, when the evidence points in the opposite direction.
preamble.friction: The superscription is one of the longest and most historically detailed in the Psalter, connecting the psalm to David's campaigns in 2 Samuel 8 and 1 Chronicles 18. The military details do not perfectly align with the biblical narratives, suggesting either a different tradition or a composite historical reference. The phrase shushan edut ('lily of testimony') may be a melody name or a reference to a specific liturgical occasion. Verses 7-14 (Hebrew) are reused almost verbatim in Psalm 108:7-14, confirming that psalm sections circulated as independent units.
preamble.connections: The military background connects to 2 Samuel 8:1-14 (David's victories over Moab, Aram, and Edom) and 2 Samuel 10 (the Aramean wars). The divine oracle dividing the land echoes the territorial allocations of Joshua 13-21. Psalm 108:7-14 reuses the oracle and concluding verses. The 'wine of staggering' image (v. 5) appears in Isaiah 51:17, 22 and Jeremiah 25:15. The fortified city (v. 11) likely refers to the Edomite stronghold of Bozrah or Sela (Petra).

verses.text_hebrew: לַ֭מְנַצֵּחַ עַל־שׁוּשַׁ֣ן עֵד֑וּת מִכְתָּ֖ם לְדָוִ֣ד לְלַמֵּֽד׃
verses.text_kjv: To the chief Musician upon Shushaneduth, Michtam of David, to teach.
verses.rendering:
For the director of music. According to "Lily of Testimony."
A miktam of David, for instruction.
translator_notes: shushan edut ('lily of testimony') is unique to this psalm and Psalm 80 (where the plural shoshannim edut appears). Whether this names a melody, a musical instrument, or a liturgical season is unknown. The word lelammedh ('for instruction, to teach') indicates the psalm was intended as a teaching tool — the congregation was meant to learn something from it, not merely sing it.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּהַצּוֹת֨וֹ ׀ אֶ֥ת אֲרַ֣ם נַהֲרַ֗יִם וְאֶת־אֲרַ֥ם צוֹבָ֑ה וַיָּ֤שׇׁב יוֹאָ֗ב וַיַּ֥ךְ אֶת־אֱד֥וֹם בְּגֵיא־מֶ֗לַח שְׁנֵ֣ים עָשָׂ֣ר אָֽלֶף׃
verses.text_kjv: when he strove with Aramnaharaim and with Aramzobah, when Joab returned, and smote of Edom in the valley of salt twelve thousand.
verses.rendering:
When he fought against Aram-Naharaim and Aram-Zobah,
and Joab returned and struck down Edom
in the Valley of Salt — twelve thousand.
translator_notes: The superscription provides the military context: simultaneous campaigns against the Aramean kingdoms to the northeast (Aram-Naharaim, 'Aram of the Two Rivers,' upper Mesopotamia; Aram-Zobah, a kingdom in modern Lebanon/Syria) and Edom to the southeast. Joab's strike against Edom in the Valley of Salt (south of the Dead Sea) killed 12,000. The text of 2 Samuel 8:13 credits David with 18,000 Edomites; the discrepancy may reflect different battle counts from the same campaign.

verses.text_hebrew: אֱלֹהִ֣ים זְנַחְתָּ֣נוּ פְרַצְתָּ֑נוּ אָ֝נַ֗פְתָּ תְּשׁ֣וֹבֵ֥ב לָֽנוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: O God, thou hast cast us off, thou hast scattered us, thou hast been displeased; O turn thyself to us again.
verses.rendering:
God, you have rejected us, broken through our lines;
you were angry — restore us!
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm opens not with praise but with accusation. Three verbs indict God: zenachtanu ('you have rejected us, cast us off'), peratstanu ('you have broken through us' — the verb parats describes a breach in a wall, a break in a battle line), and anafta ('you were angry'). The verbs are in the perfect tense — these are completed actions. God has already done this. The damage is done. Then the desperate plea: teshovev lanu ('turn back to us, restore us'). The verb shuv in the Polel form means to turn again, to reverse course. David is asking God to undo what God has done.
translator_notes: The raw honesty of blaming God for military defeat is characteristic of Israelite lament. The psalms do not protect God from accusation — they bring the accusation directly to God because God is the only one who can reverse the situation. Complaint is an act of faith: you only accuse someone you believe has the power to change things.

verses.text_hebrew: הִרְעַ֣שְׁתָּה אֶ֣רֶץ פְּצַמְתָּ֑הּ רְפָ֖ה שְׁבָרֶ֣יהָ כִי־מָֽטָה׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou hast made the earth to tremble; thou hast broken it: heal the breaches thereof; for it shaketh.
verses.rendering:
You have shaken the land and split it open;
heal its fractures, for it totters.
verses.expanded_rendering: The earthquake metaphor captures the scope of the crisis. hir'ashta erets ('you shook the land') — the defeat was not a surface event but a tectonic upheaval. petsamtah ('you split it open') — the land itself has cracked, the foundations are compromised. The plea: refah shevareha ('heal its fractures, mend its breaks') — from rapha ('to heal'), the same verb used for healing the body. The land is injured, sick, and needs the divine physician. The reason: ki matah ('for it totters, it is shaking') — the instability continues. The earthquake is not over; the aftershocks persist.
translator_notes: The land-as-body metaphor — shaken, broken, needing healing — connects to the prophetic tradition where the land suffers for the people's sin or flourishes with their faithfulness (Hosea 4:1-3, Isaiah 24:4-6). The land is not just territory but a participant in the covenant relationship.

verses.text_hebrew: הִרְאִ֣יתָה עַמְּךָ֣ קָשָׁ֑ה הִ֝שְׁקִיתָ֗נוּ יַ֣יִן תַּרְעֵלָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou hast shewed thy people hard things: thou hast made us to drink the wine of astonishment.
verses.rendering:
You have shown your people harsh things;
you have made us drink wine that staggers.
verses.expanded_rendering: God has done two things to his people: hir'itah ammekha qashah ('you have shown your people harsh, hard things') — not hidden the difficulty but displayed it, made them see it. And hishqitanu yayin tar'elah ('you have made us drink wine of staggering'). The wine of tar'elah ('staggering, reeling') is a prophetic image that recurs throughout the Hebrew Bible — a cup of divine judgment that makes the drinker stumble and fall (Isaiah 51:17, 22; Jeremiah 25:15-16). The image says: God himself has intoxicated his people with disaster. They are reeling not from enemy attack alone but from God's own hand pouring the cup. The accusation is severe and uncompromising.
translator_notes: The wine-of-staggering image is part of a broader biblical tradition of the 'cup of God's wrath' — a metaphor where divine judgment is figured as an intoxicating drink that the recipient must consume. The image will reach its fullest development in Revelation 14:10 and 16:19.

verses.text_hebrew: נָ֘תַ֤תָּה לִּירֵאֶ֣יךָ נֵּ֣ס לְהִתְנוֹסֵ֑ס מִ֝פְּנֵ֗י קֹ֣שֶׁט סֶֽלָה׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou hast given a banner to them that fear thee, that it may be displayed because of the truth. Selah.
verses.rendering:
You have given those who fear you a banner
to rally around, because of truth.
Selah.
verses.expanded_rendering: A sudden shift from accusation to acknowledgment. Even amid defeat, God has given li-re'ekha ('to those who fear you') a nes ('banner, signal, standard'). The military banner was the rallying point for troops in battle — when the line was breaking, soldiers looked for the banner to regroup. God has provided a rallying point for the faithful. The purpose: lehitnoses mippenei qoshet ('to rally/display because of truth'). qoshet ('truth, reality') is a rare word, possibly related to qeshet ('bow') — some read 'to flee from the bow,' but the more likely meaning is 'because of truth/faithfulness.' God's banner is raised not because of Israel's military strength but because of emet — the reliable truth of God's character.
translator_notes: The nes ('banner') will become a messianic image in Isaiah 11:10, 12 ('he will raise a banner for the nations'). In this context it is a military signal — the point of hope around which the scattered can regroup.

key_terms.note: The related term qoshet ('truth') appears here. The banner is raised because God is reliable — his truth gives the defeated a reason to regroup rather than surrender.

verses.text_hebrew: לְ֭מַעַן יֵחָלְצ֣וּן יְדִידֶ֑יךָ הוֹשִׁ֖יעָה יְמִינְךָ֣ וַעֲנֵֽנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: That thy beloved may be delivered; save with thy right hand, and hear me.
verses.rendering:
So that your beloved ones may be rescued,
save with your right hand and answer me!
translator_notes: yedidekha ('your beloved ones') from yadid ('beloved, dear one') — the same root in the name Jedidiah, the name God gave Solomon through Nathan (2 Samuel 12:25). Israel is identified as God's beloved — the relationship is not contractual but affectional. The right hand (yamin) is the hand of power and action throughout the Hebrew Bible.

verses.text_hebrew: אֱלֹהִ֤ים ׀ דִּבֶּ֥ר בְּקׇדְשׁ֗וֹ אֶ֫עְלֹ֥זָה אֲחַלְּקָ֥ה שְׁכֶ֑ם וְעֵ֖מֶק סֻכּ֣וֹת אֲמַדֵּֽד׃
verses.text_kjv: God hath spoken in his holiness; I will rejoice, I will divide Shechem, and mete out the valley of Succoth.
verses.rendering:
God has spoken in his sanctuary:
"I will triumph! I will divide up Shechem
and measure out the Valley of Succoth.
verses.expanded_rendering: The divine oracle begins. God speaks be-qodsho ('in his holiness' or 'from his sanctuary') — the speech comes from the place of ultimate authority. What follows is God claiming ownership of the entire land by dividing and measuring it. Shechem represents the heartland of Israel (central hill country, west of the Jordan); the Valley of Succoth represents the Transjordan territory (east of the Jordan, in the Jordan Valley). God divides Shechem (achalleqah) and measures Succoth (amadded) — the verbs are those of a landowner parceling his estate. The land is not Israel's; it is God's, and he distributes it as he chooses.
translator_notes: The oracle (vv. 8-10) is one of the few places in the Psalms where God speaks directly in first person. The format resembles a prophetic oracle delivered in a worship setting — possibly by a priest or prophet during a liturgy of lament. The claim of sovereignty over specific territories gives the oracle concrete, geographic content.

verses.text_hebrew: לִ֤י גִלְעָ֨ד ׀ וְלִ֣י מְ֭נַשֶּׁה וְאֶפְרַ֣יִם מָע֣וֹז רֹאשִׁ֑י יְ֝הוּדָ֗ה מְחֹקְקִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: Gilead is mine, and Manasseh is mine; Ephraim also is the strength of mine head; Judah is my lawgiver;
translator_notes: mechoqeq ('lawgiver, commander's staff, scepter') connects to Jacob's blessing of Judah in Genesis 49:10 ('the scepter shall not depart from Judah, nor the ruler's staff from between his feet'). God's designation of Judah as 'my scepter' confirms the Davidic royal line as God's chosen instrument of governance.

verses.text_hebrew: מוֹאָ֤ב ׀ סִ֥יר רַחְצִ֗י עַל־אֱ֭דוֹם אַשְׁלִ֣יךְ נַעֲלִ֑י עָ֝לַ֗י פְּלֶ֣שֶׁת הִתְרוֹעָֽעִי׃
verses.text_kjv: Moab is my washpot; over Edom will I cast out my shoe; Philistia, triumph thou because of me.
translator_notes: The domestic imagery is deliberately humiliating to Israel's enemies and deliberately comforting to Israel. In a psalm of military defeat, God's oracle declares that the enemies who seem so powerful are, from God's perspective, household objects. The humor is theological: panic about geopolitics is disproportionate when God treats your enemies as his washbasin.

The sandal-over-Edom gesture may also allude to the master-servant relationship: in ancient custom, a servant's job was to carry the master's sandals. God flings his sandal over Edom, treating it as his servant.

verses.text_hebrew: מִ֣י יֹ֭בִלֵנִי עִ֣יר מָצ֑וֹר מִ֖י נָחַ֣נִי עַד־אֱדֽוֹם׃
verses.text_kjv: Who will bring me into the strong city? who will lead me into Edom?
verses.rendering:
Who will bring me to the fortified city?
Who will lead me to Edom?
verses.expanded_rendering: After the oracle's confidence, reality reasserts itself. David (or the army commander) asks: mi yovileni ir matsor ('who will bring me to the fortified city?'). The ir matsor is a walled, defended stronghold — likely Bozrah or Sela (Petra), the Edomite capital cut into rock cliffs. The repetition — mi nachani ad Edom ('who will lead me to Edom?') — acknowledges that despite the oracle's grand claims, the actual military campaign against Edom has not yet succeeded. The gap between divine promise and present reality is the psalm's theological tension.
translator_notes: The fortified city of Edom was likely Sela (later Petra), a city carved into sandstone cliffs that was nearly impregnable by ancient military standards. The question 'who will bring me there?' is not rhetorical — it is a genuine military problem. Only God can crack that fortress.

verses.text_hebrew: הֲלֹֽא־אַתָּ֣ה אֱלֹהִ֣ים זְנַחְתָּ֑נוּ וְלֹא־תֵצֵ֖א אֱלֹהִ֣ים בְּצִבְאוֹתֵֽינוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Wilt not thou, O God, which hadst cast us off? and thou, O God, which didst not go out with our armies?
verses.rendering:
Have you not rejected us, God?
You do not march out with our armies, God.
verses.expanded_rendering: The complaint from verse 3 returns: halo attah Elohim zenachtanu ('have you not rejected us, God?'). The question expects the answer 'yes.' And the evidence: ve-lo tetse Elohim be-tsiv'otenu ('you do not march out with our armies'). In Israel's theology, God was supposed to go to war with his people — the Ark of the Covenant preceded the army, and God's presence determined the battle's outcome (cf. Numbers 10:35, 1 Samuel 4:3-11). God's refusal to 'go out' with the army is not mere absence but active withdrawal. The armies fight alone, which means they fight as mere humans against fortified cities — and mere humans cannot breach Petra.
translator_notes: The honesty of this verse is remarkable in a liturgical text. The psalm does not pretend things are fine. God's absence from the battlefield is stated as fact, not questioned as possibility. The psalm holds together divine sovereignty (God rules all nations in the oracle) and present divine absence (God does not march with the army). Both are true at the same time.

verses.text_hebrew: הָבָה־לָּ֣נוּ עֶזְרָ֣ת מִצָּ֑ר וְ֝שָׁ֗וְא תְּשׁוּעַ֥ת אָדָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: Give us help from trouble: for vain is the help of man.
verses.rendering:
Give us help against the enemy,
for human salvation is worthless.
verses.expanded_rendering: The petition is simple: havah lanu ezrat mi-tsar ('give us help from/against the adversary'). And the theological premise that undergirds it: shav teshu'at adam ('the salvation of humanity is worthless/futile'). shav means 'emptiness, vanity, worthlessness' — the same word from the third commandment ('do not take the LORD's name in vain/for emptiness'). Human rescue efforts — military strategy, alliances, numbers, fortifications — are shav. Not 'limited' or 'insufficient' but empty. The only help that has substance is God's. This is not fatalism but the clear-eyed recognition that the problem exceeds human capacity.
translator_notes: teshu'at adam ('human salvation') is dismissed with the strongest possible term. shav does not mean 'second-best' or 'partial' — it means empty, nothing, vapor. Human help is not a lesser option; it is no option at all when God is the one who determines the outcome.

verses.text_kjv: Through God we shall do valiantly: for he it is that shall tread down our enemies.
verses.rendering:
With God we will act with valor,
and he himself will trample our enemies.
translator_notes: The psalm's theological arc is complete: defeat (vv. 3-5) → rallying point (v. 6) → divine oracle of sovereignty (vv. 8-10) → honest acknowledgment of present weakness (vv. 11-13) → trust in God's future action (v. 14). The trajectory is not from despair to resolution but from despair to trust. The fortified city of Edom remains unconquered. But God has spoken, and that is enough.
